anvaya.meaning: toward well-wisher, friend, enemy, indifferent, mediator, hateful, kinsman

translations.verbatim_quote: A person is considered still further advanced when he regards honest well-wishers, affectionate benefactors, the neutral, mediators, the envious, friends and enemies, the pious and the sinners all with an equal mind.

A person is considered still further advanced when he regards honest well-wishers, affectionate benefactors, the neutral, mediators, the envious, friends and enemies, the pious and the sinners all with an equal mind.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 0 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Krishna extends the equanimity diagnostic from natural objects (clod-stone-gold) to relational categories (eight classes of persons + righteous/sinful).

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.9 The first line of the verse beginning with ‘benefactor,’ etc. is a single compound word. Visisyate, he excels, i.e. he is the best among all those who are established in Yoga-(a different reading is vimucyate, he becomes free); sama-buddhih, who has sameness of view, i.e.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-9-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.9 He is highly esteemed who has equal regard towards a benefactor, friend, foe, indifferent person, neutral, hateful, relative, and even towards the righteous and the unrighteous.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.9 The first line of the verse beginning with ‘benefactor,’ etc. is a single compound word. Visisyate, he excels, i.e. he is the best among all those who are established in Yoga-(a different reading is vimucyate, he becomes free); sama-buddhih, who has sameness of view, i.e.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-9-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.9 He is the highest who is equal-minded toward suhṛd (well-wisher), mitra (friend), ari (enemy), udāsīna (the unconcerned), madhyastha (mediator), dveṣya (the hated), bandhu (kinsman), the saintly and the sinful — equal in his evaluation of his own state, undisturbed by the variations of their relation to him.

positions.rendering: equal regard / equal-mindedness across all eight relational categories plus the righteous and unrighteous
equal-minded — equal in his evaluation of his own state, undisturbed by the variations of their relation to him
regards all with an equal mind

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both Shankara and Ramanuja are explicit that this is steadiness of the discriminating intellect, not endorsement of moral indifference. The yogi continues to act rightly; what is even is the inner response-shape, not the outer judgment.

literal_meaning: One who has equal intellect toward well-wishers, friends, enemies, the indifferent, the mediator, the hated, kinsmen, and even toward the righteous and the sinful — that one excels.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna extends the equanimity diagnostic from natural objects (clod-stone-gold) to relational categories (eight classes of persons + righteous/sinful). The verse names eight relational categories — well-wisher, friend, enemy, indifferent, mediator, hated, kinsman, plus saint and sinner — and claims the yogi who maintains equal intellect across all of them excels. Note: this is not endorsing moral indifference; both Shankara and Ramanuja read it as steadiness of the discriminating intellect across the relational pulls, not as absence of right-action.
